Varak Welfare Society

September 2025 - January 2026

Worked with the CTO to architect a scalable subscription engine using Django, PostgreSQL, and Razorpay, building the entire interface using React.js and TailwindCSS. This system was built to handle all payment edge cases (even if the user is not logged in).

Migrated the organization's homepage from Vue.js to React.js. This shift to a modern component architecture significantly improved mobile responsiveness and reduced initial load latency by 35%.

Contributed to enhancing the user experience by building several key frontend components, including the FAQs section, individual campaign pages, and a donor information modal. I also coded a fully dynamic navbar that could be controlled directly from the Django backend. To support the internal team, I helped in developing the frontend and backend integration for an admin dashboard that visualized database results using custom stored queries.

Implemented a secure Google OAuth 2.0 authentication system with robust input validations and error handling for signup and login flows. Additionally, I guided four other developer interns through their onboarding setup and provided active debugging support.

data.name: LocalBee
data.summary: World map to track places I have visited and store their photos (DB down).

content:
A personal travel map that pins every place I've visited - complete with photos, ratings, and notes from the trip.

Overview

LocalBee lets you log a place by entering its coordinates, name, description, rating, image links, and tags. Once added, the location is pinged directly on an interactive Leaflet-powered map, building up a visual record of everywhere you've traveled over time. Places can be updated or removed as needed, and you can filter and browse visited spots by tag.

Inspiration

I've always loved traveling, and even more, photographing the places I visit - old monuments, quiet nature trails, anything with character. Over time, those photos just piled up with no real way to look back at where a shot was taken or what I thought of the place. LocalBee started as a way to fix that. The mapping piece came together almost by accident - I came across Leaflet while looking through a senior's resume, and it turned out to be exactly the kind of lightweight, flexible library this project needed.

Key Features

  • Add a place - Log coordinates, name, description, rating, image links, and tags for any location you've visited
  • Interactive map pins - Every saved place is automatically pinged on the map using its coordinates
  • Edit or delete - Update details or remove a place entirely as your records change
  • Filter by tags - Quickly retrieve places based on the tags you've assigned them

data.name: Pokedex
data.summary: Pokedex for 8 generations of pokemon using PokeAPI.

content:
A pixel-themed Pokedex built purely on nostalgia and imagination.

Overview

Built on top of the PokeAPI, this Pokedex lets you search for any Pokémon and dive into their details - stats, type, and generation - all wrapped in a clean, pixel-art-inspired interface.

Inspiration

Pokémon was one of the first cartoons I ever watched as a kid, and it never really left - from collecting cards to grinding through FireRed and Emerald on my Game Boy, it's been a constant thread through pretty much every phase of my school life. I didn't actually plan this project out - there was no wireframe or feature list, I just imagined what a Pokedex should feel like and kept coding until it matched. It also holds a special place as the repository that earned my very first GitHub star.

Key Features

  • Search - Look up any Pokémon by name
  • Filter by type - Narrow the list down to specific Pokémon types
  • Filter by generation - Browse Pokémon from a specific generation
  • Stat charts - Visualize each Pokémon's stats using Chart.js
  • Lazy-loaded images - Sprites load progressively for a smoother browsing experience
  • Scroll to top - Quick navigation back to the top of long lists
  • Live API integration - All Pokémon data is fetched in real time from PokeAPI

data.name: Cineverse
data.summary: Movie and series collection with rating, plot & cast information using OMDb API.

content:
A movie & TV discovery app built to make binge-watch planning effortless - find it, check it out, save it for later.

Overview

Cineverse is a single place to search for movies and shows using the OMDb API, pull up their details and ratings, and save the genuinely binge-worthy ones to a personal watchlist instead of losing track of them.

Inspiration

As a fairly committed binge-watcher, I was constantly hopping between sites just to check a rating or remember the plot of something I'd half-heard about. Cineverse was my answer to that. It also pushed me technically in ways I didn't expect - working with the OMDb API taught me how to fetch and structure data efficiently, and building the modal handling and horizontal-scrolling movie list completely from scratch (no prebuilt carousel or modal library) was one of the more demanding frontend challenges I'd taken on at the time.

Key Features

  • Search - Look up movies and TV series by name
  • Detail modal - View full information for a title - plot, ratings, and more - in a custom-built modal, no library used
  • Watchlist management - Add or remove titles from your personal watchlist
  • Action notifications - Get a confirmation toast whenever a movie is added or removed from the watchlist
  • Light/dark mode - Toggle between themes based on preference

data.name: Blogeez
data.summary: Website to read, upload, edit & delete blogs with authentication.

content:
My first full-stack application - built to understand authentication and CRUD operations.

Overview

Blogeez is a blog uploading and reading platform where users can create accounts, manage their own posts, and browse content and profiles from other users. Every user profile includes a "My Blogs" section showing all posts they've uploaded.

Inspiration

Blogeez was where I moved from frontend-only projects into building a complete application, backend included. The goal was deliberate: I wanted to deeply understand how authentication and CRUD operations work end-to-end, rather than just importing a library and trusting it to work. It never made it to a deployed final version, but by the time it was done, it genuinely felt like I'd built my own miniature version of Reddit from scratch.

Key Features

  • Secure authentication - User passwords are encrypted and stored using bcrypt
  • Token-based sessions - JWT (jsonwebtoken) used to sign and verify tokens, paired with cookie-parser for cookie-based session handling
  • Full blog CRUD - Create, edit, and delete your own blog posts, while viewing posts and profiles from other users
  • Per-user blog feed - Every user profile includes a dedicated section showing all blogs they've uploaded
  • API communication - Axios used throughout for handling HTTP requests between client and server

data.name: Valorant tracker
data.summary: Valorant match history and statistics tracker.

content:
A custom Valorant stats tracker built to surface the deeper, more granular stats the popular trackers leave out.

Overview

Built on top of HenrikDev's Valorant API, this tracker pulls live player and match data and presents everything from rank progression to round-by-round breakdowns in a clean, readable dashboard - including stats most trackers don't surface, like per-weapon kill counts and per-agent ability usage.

Inspiration

I've been playing Valorant since Act 1 - what I expected to be a short-lived hype game turned into one of the most fun and competitive games I've ever played, and the climb from Silver to Ascendant introduced me to some genuinely great people along the way. The idea for this tracker came from a gap I kept running into on tracker.gg, the most popular Valorant tracking site - it didn't show things I actually wanted to see, like kills per weapon or ability usage per agent. So I built my own, and added a stat purely for fun in the process: the "Baiter" stat, tracking the max number of times a player was left alone as the last one alive while multiple enemies on their team were still up.

Key Features

  • Progress graph - Visualizes rank progression at the end of each competitive act
  • Player search - Look up any player using their name#tag
  • Profile overview - Player level, current rank, and peak rank
  • Match history - RR gained/lost, rank at the time, map played, and final score
  • Detailed 5v5 stats - Per-player breakdown including level, score, rank, kills, deaths, assists, headshots, bodyshots, legshots, AFK rounds, average credits spent, ability usage count, weapon used per kill, and the "Baiter" stat for each team
  • Round history - Win/loss outcome and bomb plant site for every round

A responsive plant nursery website built from genuine plant-care experience.

Overview

FloraGhar is a plant nursery website organized into categories like seeds, plants, pots, and accessories. Each product page includes pricing alongside real care information - light requirements, watering frequency, and the kind of space it thrives in. Filters are designed with beginners in mind, so even someone with zero plant knowledge can narrow products down to what suits the space they actually have.

Inspiration

This was my first complete frontend project, and I wanted it to be about something I actually cared about rather than the typical first-project e-commerce clone. Since 2022, I've been growing over 20 plants from saplings myself - researching soil mixtures, sunlight needs, and watering patterns for each one until they matured into full-grown plants. That hands-on experience became the foundation for FloraGhar.

Key Features

  • Multi-page layout - Organized navigation across product categories and pages
  • Search - Quickly find specific products
  • Sort & filter - Narrow products down by category or care requirements
  • Shopping cart - Add and manage products before checkout
  • Image & review sliders - Browse product photos and customer reviews in an interactive carousel

data.name: ProductWizz
data.summary: An AI-powered shopping assistant browser extension for amazon.in & flipkart.com.

content:
An AI-powered Chrome extension that answers your product questions instantly - no more endless review scrolling.

Overview

ProductWizz is a Chrome extension that scrapes product specs and reviews (capped at 100 per product) directly from Amazon India and Flipkart listings, feeds that data into a locally running LLM, and lets you chat with it about the product. Curious what people think about the camera quality, or whether the fabric pills after a few washes? Just ask. Under the hood, it uses a RAG pipeline - reviews are embedded with Sentence Transformers, stored in ChromaDB, and retrieved by LangChain to ground answers generated by a locally running Zephyr 7B model. Since everything runs locally, there's no dependency on external APIs and the extension works fully offline once a product has been extracted. A demo walkthrough is available here.

Inspiration

Anyone who's shopped online knows the drill: you find a product, the price looks right, and then comes the hard part - scrolling through hundreds of reviews trying to figure out if the battery actually lasts, if sizing runs small, or if that one defect everyone's complaining about is a dealbreaker. That frustration is what led to ProductWizz - instead of manually digging through reviews, I wanted to just ask and get a straight answer pulled from what real buyers were actually saying.

Key Features

  • Smart extraction - Pulls product details and customer reviews directly from the Amazon/Flipkart page you're viewing
  • Conversational Q&A - Ask natural-language questions and get answers grounded in actual product specs and review content
  • Persistent chat history - Conversations are saved between sessions, so you can pick up right where you left off
  • Easy reset - Clear the current chat or extract a new product whenever you want
  • Fully offline - Since the LLM runs locally, there's no internet dependency once data is extracted

data.name: LLM from scratch
data.summary: A complete implementation of GPT model like LLM from scratch with attention, training, fine-tuning and text classification capabilities.

content:
A from-scratch PyTorch implementation of a GPT-style Large Language Model - built to actually understand how the tools I use every day work under the hood.

Overview

This repository is a clean, chapter-by-chapter implementation of every core concept from Sebastian Raschka's Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch), organized so it can double as a reference for anyone else working through the same material. It covers custom tokenization, self-attention and multi-head attention, a full GPT architecture, a complete pretraining loop, and fine-tuning for both classification (SMS spam detection) and instruction-following (Alpaca-style dataset). The model was trained and fine-tuned on Google Colab - notebook here - with loss curves tracked for both fine-tuning stages. It's currently my highest-starred repository at 18 stars.

Inspiration

I'd been using LLMs almost daily for studying and planning but had no real understanding of what was happening beneath the chat interface. During a break from coursework, that curiosity turned into a project: instead of just reading about transformers, I wanted to build one myself, line by line. After researching options across forums and reviews, I landed on Raschka's book - it came highly recommended, and using PyTorch instead of TensorFlow was an added bonus since it was a framework I'd been wanting to get comfortable with anyway.

Key Features

  • Custom tokenization (src/data.py) - SimpleTokenizerV1/V2, a GPTDatasetV1 sliding-window dataset class, and configurable data loaders
  • Attention mechanisms (src/attention.py) - self-attention v1/v2, causal masking (CausalAttention), and efficient MultiHeadAttention
  • GPT architecture (src/model.py) - full GPTModel, TransformerBlock, LayerNorm, GELU, and FeedForward implementations
  • Training pipeline (src/train.py) - complete training loop, loss calculation utilities, temperature/top-k text generation, and loss plotting
  • Classification fine-tuning (src/finetune_classification.py) - SMS spam detection, converting the generative model into a classifier
  • Instruction fine-tuning (src/finetune_instructions.py) - Alpaca-style prompt formatting for instruction-following behavior
  • Chapter-mapped structure - each file corresponds directly to a chapter of the book (Ch. 2–7), making it easy to follow alongside the text
